questions.question: Explain the difference between primary, extended, and logical partitions in the MBR partition scheme.
keyPoints: Primary partition: Bootable, directly holds data, max 4 per disk
Extended partition: Cannot hold data directly, acts as container, only 1 per disk
Logical partitions: Created inside extended partition, unlimited in number
At least one partition must be primary for booting

questions.question: What is mounting? Describe three types of mounting in operating systems.
keyPoints: Mounting attaches a file system to directory tree at mount point
Manual mounting: Using 'mount' command by administrator
Automatic mounting: File systems mounted at boot via /etc/fstab
Temporary mounting: Exists only until system reboot
Network mounting: Remote file systems via NFS, SMB

questions.question: Compare MBR and GPT partition schemes on the basis of: (i) Maximum disk size supported, (ii) Number of partitions, (iii) Data redundancy.
keyPoints: MBR: 2TB max, 4 primary partitions (or 3+1 extended), no redundancy
GPT: 8ZB max, up to 128 partitions, redundant partition table
MBR uses 32-bit LBA, GPT uses 64-bit LBA
GPT includes CRC checksums for integrity verification
GPT required for UEFI systems

questions.question: What is the purpose of /etc/fstab? List at least four fields in an fstab entry.
keyPoints: /etc/fstab defines automatic mount configuration at boot
Fields: Device (UUID or /dev/sdX)
Mount point directory path
File system type (ext4, ntfs, swap)
Mount options (ro, rw, noexec, etc.)
Dump (backup flag) and Fsck (boot order)

questions.question: Explain the role of Virtual File System (VFS) in handling multiple file system types.
keyPoints: VFS provides abstraction layer between user applications and actual file systems
Offers uniform system call interface (open, read, write, close)
Supports multiple file systems: ext4, FAT32, NTFS, NFS, ISO9660
Handles translation between generic operations and FS-specific operations
Enables mounting diverse file systems seamlessly

questions.question: A system administrator needs to partition a new 4TB hard disk for a Linux system with dual-boot Windows. Design a partition scheme considering: boot requirements, swap space, root, home, and Windows partition. Justify your design.
keyPoints: GPT required (MBR limited to 2TB)
UEFI boot partition: 500MB-1GB, FAT32 for UEFI compatibility
Windows partition: Primary partition, NTFS, ~500GB
Linux boot: Separate /boot, 1GB, ext4
Swap: RAM size × 1.5 if hibernation needed, else × 0.5-1
Root (/): 50-100GB, ext4
Home (/home): Remaining space, ext4
Justify: Separation improves security, recovery, and performance

questions.question: Write and explain the Linux mount command to mount an ext4 partition (/dev/sdb1) to /mnt/data with read-write access, ignoring suid/guid, and no execution of binaries. Also explain how to make this mount permanent.
keyPoints: Command: mount -t ext4 -o rw,nosuid,noexec,nodev /dev/sdb1 /mnt/data
-t specifies file system type
-o specifies options: rw (read-write), nosuid (ignore setuid), noexec (no binaries), nodev (no devices)
For permanent mount, add to /etc/fstab
Entry format: /dev/sdb1 /mnt/data ext4 defaults,nosuid,noexec,nodev 0 2
Use UUID instead of device name for reliability
Test with 'mount -a' before reboot

questions.question: A user reports that after mounting a USB drive to /media/flash, they cannot see their original folder 'documents' that was in /media/flash. Explain what happened and how to recover access to the original folder.
keyPoints: Original folder contents are hidden, not deleted when mount overlays directory
The mounted file system completely replaces view of directory
To recover: unmount USB drive using 'umount /media/flash'
After unmount, original contents of /media/flash become visible again
Best practice: Use empty mount points for mounting
Alternative: Mount to new directory like /mnt/usb instead of existing folder
Files are safe - hidden by mount, not removed
